turns.content: What is the actual difference between using raw sysinfo crate crates versus manually parsing /proc files on Linux?

The core distinction lies in how you handle low-level file access. The sysinfo crate wraps the unsafe std::fs reading of /proc by creating safe abstractions like MemoryInfo or CpuStats structs that implement the trait for serialization, whereas manual parsing requires you to open files using std::fs::File and then decode bytes into strings before constructing Rust types yourself. You have to manage error states manually when a line doesn't match your regex pattern.
I thought I could just read /proc/meminfo as plain text and map it directly to struct fields without any parsing logic at all, right?

That is a common misconception. The file format uses arbitrary whitespace between the key name and its value, so you cannot simply split on spaces to get both parts reliably. You must parse each line by splitting on the first space to separate the label from the rest, then further strip units like 'kB' or '%' using regex patterns before converting the remaining string to an integer.
Okay, so I need to implement my own regex matching for every unit type in /proc/stat too?

Exactly. For /proc/stat you are dealing with multi-word lines where the first column is a CPU name and subsequent columns represent specific counters like context switches or interrupts. You cannot assume fixed positions; you must iterate through fields, match against known names from the Linux kernel documentation, and accumulate values into your struct only when the label matches.
That sounds tedious for every file. Is there a way to avoid writing custom parsers entirely?

Yes. The sysinfo crate provides pre-built structs like CpuStat that expose these fields directly without you needing to write regex logic or string parsing code from scratch. It handles the complexity of reading multiple lines and aggregating them into a single object.
Wait, so if I use sysinfo, do I still need to worry about permissions on /proc files?

No. The crate resolves file access issues internally by opening the descriptors safely before you interact with them. However, it is worth noting that even with crates like this, running as a non-root user will block attempts to read certain sensitive entries in /proc/stat if your process lacks the necessary capabilities.
